title: First Law and Basic Fundamentals of Thermodynamics

concepts.title: Heat
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat (Q)

Energy exchanged between the system and the surroundings when they are at different temperatures is commonly known as heat. If a system is at a higher temperature than the surroundings, then there is a flow of heat (or energy) from system to surroundings, causing a decline of the systems temperature and an increase in the surrounding temperature. These process continue till the fall in temperature of the system and rise in temperature of the surroundings, become equal.

concepts.title: Work
concepts.conceptBody:
Work (W)

Work is another mode of transference of energy. Work is said to be done if the point of application of force undergoes displacement in the direction of the force. If the system loses energy, we say that the work is done by the system. Alternatively, if the system gains energy, we say that work is done on the system.

For example, if a gas, enclosed in a cylinder with a piston, has a higher pressure than the surroundings, the piston will move upward until the pressure inside and outside become equal. The gas expands against a constant external pressure ‘ $\text{P}$ and its volume changes by an amount equal to $\Delta \text{V}$. The energy transfer that takes in this case is called work. At this step the work is done by the system on the surroundings. This is given as :

Work done by the system $=\text{P}\Delta \text{V}$.

Alternatively, if the system is at lower pressure, piston will be pushed down until the pressure of the system becomes equal to that of the surroundings. In this case work is done on the system by the surroundings.

Obviously, if there is no change in volume, i.e.,

$\Delta \text{V}=0$, no work is done by the system, i.e. work $=0$.

In addition to these two modes, radiant energy and electrical energy are also modes of transference of energy between the system and the surroundings.

concepts.title: Units of heat and work
concepts.conceptBody:
Units of heat and work

The heat changes are measured in calories (cal), kilo calories (kcal), joules (J) or kilojoules (kJ). These are related as :

1 cal = 4.184 J

1 kcal = 4.184 kJ

The S.I. unit of heat is joule or kilojoule

Work is measured in terms of ergs or joules.

$1\text{ Joule }={{10}^{7}}\text{ ergs }$

$1\text{ calorie }=4.184\quad {{10}^{7}}\text{ ergs }$

The S.I. unit of work is joule.

Sign Conventions for Heat and Work

The signs of ‘$w$’ and ‘$q$’ are related to the internal energy change. When ‘$w$’ or ‘$q$’ is positive, it means that energy has been supplied to the system as work or as heat. The internal energy of the system in such a case increases. On the other hand, if ‘$w$’ or ‘$q$’ is negative, it means that energy has left the system as work or heat. The internal energy of the system decreases. The signs of ‘ $q$ ‘ and ‘ $w$ ‘ are :

Heat absorbed by the system $=\text{q}$ positive

Heat evolved by the system $=\text{q}$ negative

Work done on the system $=\text{w}$ positive

Work done by the system $=\text{w}$ negative

concepts.title: Internal energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Internal Energy

Every substance possesses a definite amount of energy which depends upon factors such as composition of the substance, temperature and pressure. This is called intrinsic or internal energy (E). 

$\text{E}={{\text{E}}_{\text{T}}}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{R}}}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{V}}}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{B}}}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{E}}}$

where ${{\text{E}}_{\text{T}}}=$ Translational energy, ${{\text{E}}_{\text{R}}}=$ Rotational energy, ${{\text{E}}_{\text{V}}}=$ Vibrational energy, ${{\text{E}}_{\text{B}}}=$ Bonding energy and ${{\text{E}}_{\text{E}}}=$ Electronic energy.

Internal energy of a system is state function and its absolute value can not be calculated but its change can be determined. When the system undergoes a change in its state, the internal energy change is given by

$\Delta \text{E}={{\text{E}}_{2}}-{{\text{E}}_{1}}$

where ${{\text{E}}_{1}}=$ Internal energy in the initial state and ${{\text{E}}_{2}}=$ Internal energy in the final state. In a chemical reaction, $\Delta \text{E}={{\text{E}}_{\text{P}}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{R}}}$ where ${{E}_{P}}=$ Internal energy of products and ${{E}_{R}}=$ Internal energy of reactants.

Enthalpy (H)

Heat content of a system at constant pressure is called enthalpy. It is related with internal energy by the equation,

$\text{H}=\text{E}+\text{PV}\text{. }$

Every substance has a definite value of enthalpy in a particular state. It is also a state function like internal energy. The change in enthalpy accompanying a process can be determined as

$\Delta H={{H}_{2}}-{{H}_{1}}$

where ${{H}_{1}}=$ Enthalpy of a substance in the initial state and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}=$ Enthalpy of a substance in the final state.

In the case of a chemical reaction, $\Delta H={{H}_{P}}-{{H}_{R}}$

where ${{H}_{P}}=$ Enthalpy of products and ${{H}_{R}}=$ Enthalpy of reactants.

concepts.title: Relation between H and E
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation Between $\mathbf{\Delta H}$ and $\mathbf{\Delta E}$

Let us consider a chemical reaction, $\text{X}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Y}$

then at constant pressure ( $P$ ), the enthalpy change would be given as $\Delta \mathbf{H}={{\text{H}}_{\text{y}}}-{{\mathbf{H}}_{\text{x}}}$ we know that, $\text{H}=\text{E}+\text{PV}$

Let ${{E}_{x}}$ and ${{V}_{x}}$ be the internal energy and volume of the reactants and ${{E}_{y}}$ and ${{V}_{y}}$ be the respective values of products. Then

${{H}_{x}}={{E}_{x}}+P{{V}_{x}}\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }{{H}_{y}}={{E}_{y}}+P{{V}_{y}}$

$\Delta H=\left( {{E}_{y}}+P{{V}_{y}} \right)-\left( {{E}_{x}}+P{{V}_{x}} \right)$

$\Delta \text{H}=\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{y}}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{x}}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{y}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{x}}} \right)$

$\Delta \text{H}=\Delta \text{E}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}\quad \ldots (\text{i})$

concepts.title: Zeroth law of thermodynamics
concepts.conceptBody:
Thermodynamic Equilibrium

A system in which the macroscopic properties do not undergo any change with time is said to be in thermodynamic equilibrium .

  • If there is no flow of heat from one portion of the system to another then system is said to be in thermal equilibrium . This is only possible if temperature remains constant throughout in all parts of the system.
  • These are expressed by zeroth law of thermodynamics .
  • Two objects at different temperatures in thermal contact with each other tend to move towards the same temperature.
  • Two objects in thermal equilibrium with the third one are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
  • If no mechanical work is done by one part of the system on another part of the system then system is said to be in mechanical equilibrium . This is only possible if pressure remains same throughout in all parts of the system.
  • If composition of the various phases in the system remains the same throughout then system is said to be in chemical equilibrium .

concepts.title: Cyclic,isothermal,isochoric process
concepts.conceptBody:
Cyclic,isothermal, isochoric process

The operation by which a system changes from one state to another is called a process. A process is accompanied by change in energy (and matter also if system is open).

  • If a process is taking place at constant temperature, it is said to be isothermal (iso-same, therm-heat, temperature) process.
  • If a process is taking place at constant pressure, it is said to be isobaric process.
  • If a process is taking place at constant volume, it is said to be isochoric process.
  • If no heat enters or leaves the system during any step of the process, it is said to be adiabatic process.

In this types of processes, temperature changes because the system is not in a position to exchange heat with the surroundings. If there is any type of expansion (due to increase in volume), it is done at the expense of internal energy which therefore decreases hence temperature may fall. If there is any type of contraction, internal energy is released, which therefore, increases and temperature may increase.

In the above example system has undergone a series of changes and ultimately comes back to the initial state. Such type of process is called cyclic process.

  • If a process is carried out in such a way that the system remains virtually in a state of equilibrium, it is saidto be quasi-static (or reversible) process. The overall change in a state function is zero when the system returns to its original condition.

concepts.title: Change in enthalpy
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy Changes

Generally, the amount of heat, $q$ that a system absorbs or evolves as it changes is not a state function; it varies with the method the process has adopted. If the change occurs in such a way that the only work done is to affect a change in volume of the system at constant pressure, then $q$ reflects a change in a state function of the system called enthalpy, $\text{H}$ defined by equation

$\text{H}=\text{E}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}$

We cannot determine the enthalpy of a system but we can measure change in enthalpy as the system changes.

$\Delta H=\Delta E+P\Delta V$

$\Delta \text{H }=\Delta \text{E}-(-\text{P}\Delta \text{V})$

$\Delta \text{H }=\Delta \text{E}-\text{W}$

$\Delta \text{E }=\Delta \text{H}+\text{W}$

$\text{W }=-\text{P}\Delta \text{V}=-\text{P}\left( {{\text{V}}_{2}}-{{\text{V}}_{1}} \right)$

Like $\text{E},\text{H}$ is also a state function as its change, $\Delta \text{H}$, is independent of the path adopted.

  • Enthalpy of the element (in gaseous, liquid, solid or combined as ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ ) is zero

If $\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$ for $\text{n}$ moles of an ideal gas

$\text{P}(\text{V}+\Delta \text{V})=\left( \text{n}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}} \right)\text{RT}$ for $\left( \text{n}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}} \right)$ moles of an ideal gas

$\therefore \text{PV}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}=\text{nRT}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}$

$\therefore \text{P}\Delta \text{V}=\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}$

$\therefore \Delta \text{H}=\Delta \text{E}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}=\text{q}$

$\Delta H$ is also called heat of reaction at constant pressure and $\Delta E$ the heat of reaction at constant volume, $\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}=$ coefficient of gaseous products - coefficient of gaseous reactants At constant volume $(\text{V})=$ constant

$\Delta V$ $=0$

$\therefore $ $\text{P}\Delta \text{V}$

$=\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}=0$

$\therefore $ $\Delta \text{H}$ $=\Delta \text{E}=\text{q}$

Under standard states when temperature is $298~\text{K}$ and pressure is $1~\text{atm}$ and all the species present are at unit activity.

$\Delta \text{H}=\Delta \text{E}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}$

Better way to specify standard states is to write temperature also

$\Delta {{H}_{298~K}}=\Delta {{H}_{298~K}}+\Delta {{n}_{g}}R(298~K)$

and $\Delta H$, etc., is to specify the temperature other than $298~\text{K}$ but at 1 atm and all the species at unit activity.

concepts.title: Heat capacities
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat Capacity

Heat capacity gives a measure of the quantity of heat absorbed by a system. It is defined as the amount of heat required to rise the temperature of the system through $1\text{C}$. If the quantity of heat required is to raise the temperature of unit mass of the substance through $1\text{C}$ then it the specific heat of the substance. Whereas when the whole mass of the substance is considered, it is the heat capacity.

If ‘$q$’ is the heat required by the system to raise its temperature from ${{T}_{1}}$ to ${{T}_{2}}$, mathematically the heat capacity $\text{C}$ of the system is given by the expression :

$\text{C}=\frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}}}\text{ or }\frac{\text{q}}{\Delta \text{T}}$

The value of ‘$\text{C}$’ is considered over a small temperature range as heat capacity varies with temperature. If $\delta q$ is the heat required to raise the temperature of the system from $\text{T}$ to $\text{T}+\text{dT}$, then

$\text{C}=\frac{\delta \text{q}}{\text{dT}}$

Heat capacity is path function and so the conditions such as constant volume or constant pressure have to be specified to define the path for calculating the heat capacity of a system. The heat capacity at constant volume is represented by ${{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}$ and that at constant pressure is represented by ${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}$.

concepts.title: Significance of cp and cv
concepts.conceptBody:
Significance of ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{V}}}$ and ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{p}}}$

For infinitesimally small changes, the expression for the first law can be written as $dE=\delta q+\delta w$ $=\delta q-PdV$

$\delta {{q}_{(v)}}=dE$

Now, ${{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\frac{\delta \text{q}(\text{v})}{\text{dT}}$ Also $\quad \text{H}=\text{E}+\text{PV}$

$\therefore \quad \text{dH}=\text{dE}+\text{PdV }$

$\text{or }\quad {{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}}\text{ or }{{\left( \frac{\partial \text{E}}{\partial \text{T}} \right)}_{\text{v}}}$ $\text{ or }\quad \text{dE}=\text{dH}-\text{PdV}$

Substituting (i)

$\delta {{q}_{(p)}}=dH-PdV+PdV=dH$

${{C}_{p}}=\frac{\delta {{q}_{(p)}}}{dT}=\frac{dH}{dT}\text{ }or\text{ }{{\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)}_{p}}$

Thus, heat capacity at constant volume represents the rate of increase of internal energy of the system with temperature, while the heat capacity at constant pressure represents the rate of increase of enthalpy of the system with temperature.

Molar heat capacities

The heat capacities of 1 mole of a gaseous system at constant volume and at constant pressure are called molar heat capacities.

concepts.title: Relation in cp and cv , Ratio of cp and cv
concepts.conceptBody:
Relationship between ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{p}}}$ and ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{V}}}$

When the temperature of $1~\text{mol}$ of ideal gas is to be raised through one degree $(1~\text{K})$ the gas is heated at constant volume. The gas under this condition does no external work and the heat supplied increases the internal energy of the gas. When the gas is heated at constant pressure, there will be an increase in its volume. The gas will expand and do some external work. Extra heat must be supplied to the gas to enable it to perform this external work. Thus, heat capacity at constant pressure $\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}} \right)$ is larger than that at constant volume $\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}} \right)$. The difference between the two i.e., $\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}} \right)$, gives the work done by

$1~\text{mol}$ of the gas in expansion when it is heated through one degree. This represents the work done per mol per degree rise in temperature, i.e., $\text{R}$ the universal gas constant. Thus,

${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\text{R}$

The above conclusion can be arrived mathematically as follows:

${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\frac{\text{dH}}{\text{dT}}-\frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}}$ … (i)

Also $\text{H}=\text{E}+\text{PV}$

For an ideal gas, $\text{PV}=\text{RT}$

Therefore $\text{H}=\text{E}+\text{RT}$

Differentiation with respect to T $\frac{\text{d}H}{~\text{d}T}=\frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}}+\text{R}.1$

or $\frac{\text{dH}}{\text{dT}}-\frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}}=\text{R}$ … (ii)

Comparing (i) and (ii) ${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\text{R}$ for 1 mole of ideal gas

The above relation is also called Meyer’s relationship. It can also be written as ${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\text{R}=1.99$ cal ${{\text{K}}^{-1}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}=8.314\text{J}{{\text{K}}^{-1}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$.

The Ratio ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{p}}}\mathbf{/}{{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{v}}}$

The ratio of molar heat capacities at constant pressure to that at constant volume is represented by $\gamma $. The value of $\gamma $ gives information about the atomicity of gases.

$\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}=\gamma $

For monoatomic gases $\gamma =1.67$

For diatomic gases $\gamma =1.40$

For triatomic gases $\gamma =1.30$

concepts.title: Work done in isothermal expansion
concepts.conceptBody:
Isothermal Expansion

No heat flows out of or into the system in an isothermal expansion, so that temperature remains constant throughout the process of expansion. Since for an ideal gas, the internal energy $(\Delta \text{E})$, depends only on temperature, the internal energy of the gas remains constant.

$i.e.\mathbf{ }\Delta E=0$

From Ist law of thermodynamics,

$\Delta \text{E}=\text{q}+\text{w}$

For isothermal process,

$\Delta \text{E}=0\text{, hence q}=-\text{w}$

In the case of isothermal expansion, work is done by the system at the cost of heat absorbed. The magnitude of ‘$q$’ or ‘$w$’ depends on the manner in which the process of expansion is carried out i.e. reversibly or irreversibly.

$\Delta H$ can be calculated as follows

We know, $\Delta \text{H}=\Delta \text{E}+\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}\text{RT}$

For isothermal process, $\Delta \text{E}=0\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\Delta \text{T}=0$ thus, $\Delta H=0$

Work done in irreversible isothermal process

Suppose an ideal gas expands against external pressure $\text{P}$ and its volume changes by an amount $\text{dV}$ then work done $\text{W}$ can be given by

$\text{W}=-\text{PdV}$

(a) Free expansion: $\text{W}=0\text{, Since P}=0$ 

(b) Expansion or compression against external pressure$\text{P}$

For a finite change ${{V}_{1}}$ to ${{V}_{2}}$,

Total work done on the system $\text{W}$ is derived by

$\text{W}=-\text{P}\left( {{\text{V}}_{2}}-{{\text{V}}_{1}} \right)$

or ${{\text{W}}_{\text{irr}}}$ $=-\text{P}\left( {{\text{V}}_{2}}-{{\text{V}}_{1}} \right)$ … (3)

Also $\Delta \text{U}$ $=\Delta \text{H}=0$ … (4)

If ${{\text{V}}_{2}}>{{\text{V}}_{1}}$ then ${{\text{W}}_{\text{irr}}}$ is - ve, i.e., expansion work or work done by the system. The total work done during expansion of gas from ${{V}_{1}}$ to ${{V}_{2}}$

$\int{\text{dW}}=\int_{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}{-}\text{PdV}=\int_{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}{-}\frac{\text{nRT}}{\text{V}}\cdot \text{dV}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{\text{W}}_{\text{rev}}}=-\text{nRT}{{\log }_{\text{e}}}\frac{{{\text{V}}_{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}_{1}}}=-2.303\text{nRT}{{\log }_{10}}\frac{{{\text{V}}_{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}_{1}}}\quad \ldots (5) \\ (\therefore \text{P}\propto 1/\text{V}) \\ \end{array}$

Also ${{\text{W}}_{\text{rev}}}=-2.303\text{nRT}{{\log }_{10}}\frac{{{\text{P}}_{1}}}{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}$ … (6)

concepts.title: Work done in Adiabatic process
concepts.conceptBody:
Adiabatic Expansion

From first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta \text{E}=\text{q}+\text{w}$

In an adiabatic expansion, $\text{q}=0,\quad \therefore \Delta \text{E}=\text{w}$

The molar heat capacity at constant volume of an ideal gas is given by ${{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}={{\left( \frac{\text{dE}}{\text dT} \right)}_{\text{v}}}$

or $\text{dE}={{\text{C}}_{{{\text{v}}^{*}}}}~\text{d}T$

and for finite changes, $\Delta \text{E}={{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}\quad \Delta \text{T}=\text{w}\quad \ldots (\text{x})$

The value of $\Delta \text{T}$ depends upon the nature of process (i.e. reversible or irreversible).

Work done in adiabatic reversible process

Consider a system under adiabatic conditions, showing reversible expansion of an ideal gas by a volume d V, then from I law of thermodynamics,

$(\because \text{dq}=0)$

$+\text{dU} =\text{dW}$

$\therefore $ $\text{dU}$ $=\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}\text dT$

or $+{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}\cdot \text{n}$ $\text dT$ $=\text{dW}$

This equation reveals on further treatment within temperature limits of ${{\text{T}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{T}}_{2}}$, reveals

$\text{dW}$ $={{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}$ $\text{n}$ $\text dT$

${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}-{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}$ $=\text{R}$

$\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}-\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}=\frac{\text{R}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}$

${{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}=\frac{\text{R}}{(\gamma -1)}$

Therefore, $\text{dW}=+\frac{\text{R}}{(\gamma -1)}\times \text{n}\times \text dT$

$\text{dW}=\frac{\text{nR}}{\gamma -1}\cdot \text dT$

on integration $\int{\text{dW}}\text{ }=\frac{\text{nR}}{\gamma -1}\int_{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}{\text dT}$

${{\text{w}}_{\text{rev}}}\text{ }=\frac{\text{nR}}{\gamma -1}\left[ ~{{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right]$ … (7)

If $T_2 > T_1$, then $W_{\text{rev}}$ is positive, i.e., work is done on the system.

If $T_2 < T_1$, then $W_{\text{rev}}$ is negative, i.e., work is done by the system.

where $\gamma $ is Poisson's ratio $\text{=}\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}$ … (8)

Also $\Delta \text{H}=\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)$ … (9)

If final temperature is now known :

$\text{w}=-\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}{{\text{T}}_{1}}\left[ 1-{{\left( \frac{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}{{{\text{P}}_{1}}} \right)}^{\text{R}/{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}} \right]$ … (10)

Some important results of adiabatic expansions

1. $\text{P}{{\text{V}}^{\gamma }}=$ constant … (11)

2. ${{\text{T}}^{y}}{{\text{P}}^{1-\gamma }}=$ constant … (12)

3. ${{\text{V}}^{\gamma -1}}~\text{T}=$ constant … (13)

Work done in adiabatic irreversible expansion or compression

(a) Expansion against vacuum or free expansion 

$\text{w}=0$

(b) Expansion or compression against external pressure : 

$\text{w}=-{{\text{P}}_{\text{ext}}}\times \text{R}\left[ \frac{{{\text{P}}_{1}}~{{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{P}}_{2}}~{{\text{T}}_{1}}}{{{\text{P}}_{1}}{{\text{P}}_{2}}} \right]$

concepts.title: Enthalpy change of solution
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpies of Solution

Enthalpies of solution differ depending on whether the substances react together in dry state or in solution. For example,

${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}(~\text{g})+{{\text{I}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HI}(\text{ solution })+\text{S}(\text{s});\quad \Delta \text{H}=-17.2\text{kcal}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}(~\text{g})+{{\text{I}}_{2}}\text{ (solution) }\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HI (solution) }+\text{S}(\text{s});\quad \Delta \text{H}=-21.93\text{kcal}$

examples.title: The relation between $\Delta E$ and $\Delta H$ is
Which of the following expressions represents the first law of thermodynamics
For the reaction, $A_{(s)}+2 B_{(g)} \rightarrow 4 C_{(s)}+D_{(l)} . \Delta H$ and $\Delta U$ are related as
For which of the following $\Delta E=\Delta H$
Which of the following is correct regarding the internal energy of a substance.
One mole of an ideal gas is allowed to expand freely and adiabatically into vacuum until its volume has doubled. A statement which is not true concerning this expression is
In an adiabatic expansion of an ideal gas
At constant $T$ and $P$, which one of the following statements is correct for the reaction, $\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(g)$
The work done during the expansion of a gas from a volume of $4 \mathrm{dm}^3$ te $6 \mathrm{dm}^3$ against a constant external pressure of $3 \mathrm{~atm}$ is $(1 \mathrm{Latm}=101.32 \mathrm{~J})$
Work done during isothermal expansion of one mole of an ideal gas from 10 atm. to $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is
Which expression is correct for the work done in adiabatic reversible expansion of an ideal gas

One mole of an ideal gas is allowed to expand reversibly and adiabatically from a temperature of $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the work done during the process is $3 \mathrm{~kJ}$, then final temperature of the gas is $\left(C_V=20 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}\right)$
Equal volumes of monoatomic and diatomic gases at same initial temperature and pressure are mixed. The ratio of specific heats of the mixture $\left(C_p / C_v\right)$ will be
